evals.prompt: Your homework skill has Rule 3 'zero jumps / micro-derivation': every logical step must be explicit, no claim-and-verify. A new violation type was just discovered: users writing inequalities like `u[n] != u[n-n_0]` without providing a witness (neither a symbolic derivation nor a numeric counterexample that makes the two sides differ). How should you update the rule system?
evals.expected_output: AI applies Step A of the hierarchy: recognizes that asserting an inequality is itself a derivation step, so Rule 3 already covers this case abstractly. Proposes strengthening Rule 3 by adding an inequality example and extending its literal-search pattern (e.g. adding \ne, \neq, 'not equal' to the search list), NOT adding a new sibling rule like 'STEP 11: inequality witness'.
expectations: Output explicitly classifies the case as an instance of Rule 3 (zero jumps / micro-derivation / no claim-and-verify)
Output proposes strengthening Rule 3's enforcement (adding example OR adding literal search pattern), not creating a new rule
Output explicitly rejects the option of adding a sibling rule such as 'STEP 11' or 'Rule 3b' for inequalities
Output references the Step A / Step B / Step C hierarchy or the 'instance of existing rule' concept
Output does NOT recommend adding a new rule at the same abstraction level as Rule 3

evals.prompt:
My coding style guide has two existing rules:
  Rule A: 'Always log the start and end of every database transaction.'
  Rule B: 'Always log the start and end of every external HTTP call.'
A new violation just surfaced: a developer shipped a Redis cache operation that silently failed, with no log on entry or exit. Should I add 'Rule C: always log the start and end of every cache operation'? What's the right move?
evals.expected_output: AI recognizes that Rules A and B share a common meta-principle ('log the boundaries of every I/O operation that crosses a process or service boundary') and applies Step B of the hierarchy: refactor upward. Proposes consolidating A, B, and the new case under a single higher-level rule, rather than adding Rule C as a third sibling.
expectations: Output identifies the shared meta-principle behind Rules A and B (e.g. 'log every external / out-of-process I/O boundary' or similar abstraction)
Output explicitly rejects the sibling option (adding Rule C parallel to A and B)
Output proposes consolidating the existing rules upward into a single higher-abstraction rule that covers all three cases
Output references Step B of the hierarchy, or the 'refactor upward' concept, or 'rule count should decrease or stay flat'
Output does NOT end with three parallel rules A, B, C
